request_for_quotation.client_information:
Client: Northern Lights Custom Cabinets Inc. is a mid-sized, privately owned custom cabinetry and millwork manufacturer based in Kitchener, Ontario. The company designs, builds, and installs custom kitchen cabinets, bathroom vanities, built-in shelving, and commercial millwork for offices and retail spaces across Southern Ontario.

Ownership & History:
- The business was founded in 2009 by Mark Thompson (President) and his spouse Laura Thompson (VP, Finance). Mark is a Red Seal–certified cabinetmaker with over 20 years of experience in the industry. Laura has a background in accounting and previously worked in a mid-sized public accounting firm.
- The company started as a small 3-person shop focusing on residential kitchen cabinetry. Over time, they expanded into commercial projects (small offices, retail build-outs, and dental clinics) and now employ a mix of production staff, installers, and administrative employees.
- The business has grown steadily through referrals from contractors, interior designers, and repeat clients. They have invested in modern CNC equipment, dust collection systems, and a well-organized finishing area to improve safety and productivity.

Business Operations:
- Main facility: 18,000 sq. ft. leased industrial unit with a production floor, finishing/paint booth area, small showroom, and administrative offices.
- Regular hours: Monday to Friday, 7:00 a.m. to 4:00 p.m. for production staff; 8:30 a.m. to 5:00 p.m. for office staff. Occasional overtime and Saturday work during peak project periods.
- Primary activities include: design and drafting, cabinet and millwork fabrication, finishing/spraying, on-site delivery, and installation at customer locations.
- Geographic service area: Generally within a 150 km radius of Kitchener, including Waterloo, Cambridge, Guelph, Hamilton, Mississauga, and the western GTA.

Workforce Profile:
- Total employees: 32 (including 2 owners).
  - 20 full-time production employees (cabinetmakers, machine operators, finishers).
  - 6 full-time installers working on job sites.
  - 4 office/administrative staff (including HR/payroll, project coordinator, and designer).
  - 2 owners actively involved in day-to-day operations (Mark – operations/production; Laura – finance/HR).
- Average age of employees: mid-30s to early 40s. Mix of long-tenured staff and newer apprentices.
- Turnover has been relatively low; several employees have been with the company for 7+ years.

Health & Safety Practices:
- The company maintains an up-to-date health and safety manual and conducts monthly safety meetings and quarterly formal safety training (e.g., WHMIS, lockout/tagout, safe lifting, ladder safety, and proper respirator use in the finishing area).
- Personal protective equipment (PPE) is mandatory in the shop: safety glasses, hearing protection, steel-toe boots, and dust masks/respirators as required.
- All CNC machinery and table saws have appropriate guards and emergency stop features. New hires receive machine-specific safety training before being allowed to operate equipment independently.
- Installers receive training on safe driving practices, fall protection when working on ladders or scaffolding, and how to handle heavy material safely on client sites.

Group Benefits Objectives:
- The company is seeking to maintain a competitive and comprehensive benefits plan that helps with employee attraction and retention, especially in a labour market where skilled tradespeople are in high demand.
- They want to preserve core benefits (life, AD&D, LTD, health, and dental) but are open to modest adjustments in co-insurance levels or deductibles to keep premiums sustainable.
- They place a high value on paramedical coverage (physiotherapy, chiropractic, massage therapy) due to the physical nature of the work and are interested in ensuring good coverage for prescription medications, including for chronic conditions.

Compensation & Contributions:
- The employer currently pays the majority of the group benefits premium (approximately 75%), while employees contribute the remaining 25% via payroll deductions.
- Employees are not given the option to waive coverage if they are eligible and full-time, except in very limited cases where they have spousal coverage and sign a waiver for health and dental only.

Claims & Experience Notes:
- Historically, the group has had moderate health and dental claims. There has been some utilization for musculoskeletal issues (back, shoulder, and knee problems) among production and installation staff.
- There is currently one long-term disability claim (detailed in the Employee Off Work section) related to a non-work-related back condition that required surgery. The employee has been off work for several months but is expected to attempt a gradual return to work in a modified duties role.
- Dental claims are fairly typical, with a mix of routine cleanings and some restorative work. There have been a few orthodontic claims for employees’ children.

Reasons for Marketing the Plan:
- The current carrier (Maple Leaf Benefits Insurance) has issued above-trend renewal increases for the past two years. The most recent renewal effective 2025-04-01 came in at a higher rate than expected, especially on the health and dental lines.
- The owners are concerned about long-term affordability and wish to test the market to ensure that rates and plan design are competitive with similar employers in the manufacturing and construction trades.
- They are also interested in improved digital tools (online claims submission, mobile app, and better reporting) and stronger support for mental health resources, such as an Employee Assistance Program (EAP) and coverage for psychologists or clinical counsellors.

Additional Underwriting Considerations:
- No known pending mass layoffs, business closures, or major downsizing. The company has a stable backlog of work for the next 9–12 months and is cautiously optimistic about continued demand in the renovation and light commercial construction sectors.
- There are no anticipated changes to ownership structure in the near term; Mark and Laura intend to remain actively involved for at least the next 5–7 years.
- The company has not had more than two different group benefits carriers in the last five years. They moved to the current carrier three years ago after outgrowing a small association plan.
- The employer is willing to consider a small waiting period for new hires (e.g., 3 months) if it helps control costs, but they prefer not to lengthen the waiting period beyond that.

In summary, Northern Lights Custom Cabinets Inc. is a stable, growing custom cabinetry manufacturer with a predominantly blue-collar workforce, a strong safety culture, and a desire to maintain a comprehensive yet sustainable benefits plan that supports both physical and mental health needs for their employees and their families.

client_questions.nature_of_business: Northern Lights Custom Cabinets Inc. is a custom cabinetry and millwork manufacturer based in Kitchener, Ontario. The company designs, builds, finishes, delivers, and installs custom kitchen cabinets, bathroom vanities, built-in shelving, and commercial millwork for residential homeowners, general contractors, and small commercial clients (e.g., offices, clinics, and retail stores). Core activities include wood cutting, CNC machining, assembly of cabinets and millwork, sanding and finishing (including spray finishing in a controlled booth), hardware installation, and on-site installation at customer locations. Approximately 70% of work is residential (kitchens, bathrooms, built-ins) and 30% is light commercial. All work is performed by in-house employees; no subcontracted installation crews are used for core services.
client_questions.if_yes_please_specify_seasonal_or_contra:
The company employs a small number of seasonal and contract workers during peak construction and renovation periods (typically April through October). On average, they bring on 3–4 temporary employees each year:

- Seasonal shop helpers: 2 individuals hired on 4–6 month contracts to assist with general labour in the shop, such as material handling, basic sanding, and packaging finished products for delivery. These workers are supervised closely and are not permitted to operate complex machinery without proper training.
- Seasonal installer helper: 1 individual hired on a 5–6 month contract to assist the full-time installation crews with loading/unloading cabinets, carrying materials into client homes or offices, and basic installation support under the direction of a senior installer.

Seasonal and contract staff are typically college students in woodworking or construction-related programs, or individuals with some relevant experience seeking longer-term employment. They are treated as part of the team and receive safety training and PPE. Historically, one or two seasonal employees per year are offered full-time permanent positions if there is sufficient work and they demonstrate good performance.

All seasonal and contract employees who meet eligibility criteria (full-time hours and contract duration) are offered participation in the group benefits plan, consistent with company policy, after completion of the standard waiting period.
client_questions.relationship_and_household:
The ownership group consists of Mark Thompson (President) and his spouse Laura Thompson (VP, Finance). They live in the same household and are both active in the business. Aside from the owners, there are a few family relationships among staff, but they represent significantly less than 50% of the total employee group:

- One pair of siblings: Daniel Miller (Cabinetmaker) and his younger brother Jason Miller (Installer). They do not live in the same household; each has his own residence.
- One parent–child relationship: Robert Chan (Senior Cabinetmaker) and his adult daughter Emily Chan (Junior Designer). They also live separately; Emily rents an apartment in downtown Kitchener, while Robert lives with his spouse in a nearby suburb.

These family relationships are disclosed for clarity, but overall, fewer than 10% of employees are related to each other, and there is no extended family concentration that would materially increase risk from an underwriting perspective. The majority of employees are unrelated individuals hired through standard recruitment channels (job postings, trade schools, and referrals from non-family staff).

employee_off_work_table.nature_of_disability: Non-work-related lower back injury resulting from a herniated disc. The employee experienced severe lower back pain and radiating leg pain after lifting a heavy piece of furniture at home. Following medical assessment and imaging (MRI), he underwent back surgery (lumbar microdiscectomy) in late March 2025. Post-surgery, his physician recommended an extended period of recovery and physiotherapy before returning to physically demanding work such as heavy lifting, prolonged standing, and repetitive bending or twisting.
employee_off_work_table.prognosis: Treating physician expects a gradual improvement over 6–12 months post-surgery, provided the employee adheres to physiotherapy and avoids heavy manual labour during the early recovery period. Current prognosis (as of 2025-06) is cautiously positive. It is anticipated that Michael may be able to attempt a graduated return to work on modified duties (e.g., light bench work, quality checks, and training/apprenticeship supervision) within 3–4 months, depending on his progress in physiotherapy. Full return to unrestricted cabinetmaking duties may or may not be feasible; his long-term role may need to be adjusted to focus more on supervisory or lighter physical tasks to prevent re-injury.
employee_off_work_table.life_waiver_approved: Life waiver of premium has been submitted and is currently under review with the existing carrier. Preliminary documentation has been provided, but final approval has not yet been confirmed in writing as of the date of this quotation request.

client_questions.if_not_explain_why_benefits_not_same_as_:
The quoted benefits are intended to be broadly comparable to the current plan but not identical. The employer is open to certain design changes to help manage premium costs and improve the overall value of the plan:

1. Health and Drug Coverage:
   - The current plan has 100% reimbursement for most prescription drugs with a low per-prescription dispensing fee cap. The employer is willing to consider 90% co-insurance with a modest annual deductible to help reduce premiums, provided that common and chronic medications remain affordable for employees.
   - The employer would like to explore adding or enhancing coverage for mental health practitioners (e.g., psychologists, social workers, clinical counsellors), even if it means slightly lower co-insurance on some paramedical services.

2. Paramedical Services:
   - At present, physiotherapy, chiropractic, and massage therapy are covered at 80% up to a combined annual maximum of $750 per person. Due to the physical nature of the work, the employer would like to increase the combined maximum to at least $1,000 per person, potentially with 80% reimbursement, to better support employees with musculoskeletal issues.

3. Dental Coverage:
   - The current dental plan provides 100% basic coverage and 50% major coverage, with a combined annual maximum of $1,500 per person and no orthodontic coverage for children. The employer is open to maintaining similar levels but is willing to adjust co-insurance (e.g., 90% basic instead of 100%) if this helps control cost. They would like quotes both with and without limited orthodontic coverage for dependent children.

4. Disability Benefits:
   - The current LTD plan has a 120-day elimination period and a benefit of 66.7% of monthly earnings to a maximum of $5,000. The employer is open to reviewing alternative elimination periods (e.g., 90 or 180 days) if this results in a more sustainable premium, as long as employees are not left with a significant income gap.

5. Digital Tools and EAP:
   - The current carrier’s mobile app and online tools are somewhat limited. The employer is interested in a plan that includes a strong Employee Assistance Program (EAP) and user-friendly digital claim submission, even if this involves small adjustments to other benefits.

Because of these desired enhancements and trade-offs, the requested quotation is similar in scope to the current plan but not identical. The employer is willing to consider slight reductions in some areas (e.g., co-insurance or deductibles) to fund improvements in mental health, paramedical, and digital support features.
